glossaryTerms.definition: Polymer clay with a clear or semi-clear base that allows light to pass through after curing, used to create depth effects when layered with coloured inclusions.
Small decorative elements such as dried flower petals, foil scraps, or glitter embedded inside or on the surface of polymer clay before curing.
The process of hardening polymer clay by baking it in a domestic oven at the temperature specified by the manufacturer, typically 110-130 degrees C.

content.text: A translucent panel pendant traps small objects inside a slab of clear
translucent clay
so they appear suspended like specimens in glass. Dried flower petals, tiny foil scraps, or coloured
inclusions
all work. The finished pendant holds light well and looks different depending on whether you view it against a bright or dark background. The piece is decorative only and is not food-safe.

materials.name: Dried flower petals or leaves (fully dry)
Craft foil scraps or coloured clay offcuts
Jump ring and necklace cord or chain

tools.name: Pottery rolling pin or smooth glass bottle
Baking tray lined with greaseproof paper

content.text: Preparation
Prepare your inclusions before touching the clay. Flower petals and leaves must be completely dry; any residual moisture will create steam during
curing

and produce bubbles inside the panel. Press-dried petals from a flower press or book are ideal. Warm the translucent clay in your hands for 3-4 minutes until fully pliable. Preheat the oven to 110-130 degrees C.

Divide the translucent clay into two equal portions. Roll each into a flat slab about 3 mm thick. Both slabs should be slightly larger than the final pendant size you want, so there is excess to trim after assembling.

Lay the first slab flat on your work surface. Arrange your petals, foil pieces, or coloured clay offcuts on top of it. Aim for a balanced but informal arrangement rather than a symmetrical one. Leave about 5 mm clear around the outer edge of the panel area.

Lay the second slab on top of the inclusions. Press down firmly but gently from the centre outward to push out any air pockets. Avoid pressing so hard that the inclusions get squashed flat; you want them to retain some three-dimensional presence.

Roll the rolling pin lightly across the top of the sandwich one or two times to bond the two layers together. The finished thickness should be about 5-6 mm. Translucent clay at this thickness allows light through while giving the panel enough body to handle without bending.

Cut the pendant shape from the bonded slab using the needle tool or craft knife. A rectangle with slightly rounded corners, an oval, or a simple teardrop all suit the encased effect. Cut with smooth, continuous strokes to avoid distorting the clay and shifting the inclusions inside.
Push the eye pin gently into the top edge of the pendant, embedding at least 6 mm inside the clay. Smooth around the entry point.

Place on the lined baking tray and bake at 110-130 degrees C for 25-30 minutes. Translucent clay benefits from a slightly longer bake than opaque clays to reach full clarity. Cool in the oven with the door ajar.
Once fully cool, the panel will be noticeably clearer than it appeared raw. Attach the jump ring and cord to complete the pendant.
What success looks like

The cured panel is clear with a slight amber tint (normal for translucent clay). The inclusions are visible inside the clay and appear suspended in depth. No large air bubbles are trapped inside. The edges are smooth and the eye pin is firmly held.
Common mistakes
Using fresh petals that are not fully dried: moisture trapped inside creates steam during baking and leaves bubbles around the inclusion.
Trapping large air pockets when laying the second slab: work from the centre out when pressing the slabs together to push air to the edges.
Underbaking the panel: a pale, milky appearance that does not clear on cooling usually means insufficient bake time. Rebake for a further 10-15 minutes.

items.cause: Air pockets around the inclusion or slight moisture in the petal
items.fix: Use a needle tool to pierce any surface bubbles on the raw clay before baking. Ensure all botanical inclusions are thoroughly dried before embedding.

items.problem: Panel appears milky and opaque after curing
items.cause: Underbaked or oven temperature too low
items.fix: Return the piece to the oven for an additional 15 minutes at the correct temperature. Allow to cool fully before assessing; the clay clarifies as it cools.

items.problem: Eye pin pulls out when weight is applied
items.fix: The pin must go at least 8 mm into the pendant body. For a fully cured piece where the pin has already failed, re-drill the entry point with a needle tool if soft, then use strong jewellery adhesive to secure.
